{{Infobox noble | name = Kamitsukeno no Tamichi | image = Kamitsukenu-Tamichi.jpg | image_size = 220px | caption = {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}’s depiction of Tamichi in ''{{ill|Zenken Kojitsu|ja|前賢故実}}'' | era = {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Tamichi General | honorific_suffix = | deified_as = | grave = | rank = | allegiance = {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}} | clan = {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|下毛野氏|lt=Shimotsukeno clan}}, ancestor of the Tomi no Muraji | parents = {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}}<ref name="nakata">''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}''</ref> | siblings = {{ill|Takehase no Mikoto|ja|竹葉瀬命}}<ref name="nakata"/> | spouse = | notable_works = }}
'''Kamitsukeno no Tamichi''' (田道, birth date unknown - 55th year of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}’s reign) was a warrior of the Nintoku court. His clan and title are not described in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', but the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' refers to him as '''Tamichi no Kimi''' (田道公) with the kabane title of ''Kimi''.
== Overview ==
In the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} neglected its tribute obligations. Tamichi’s elder brother, {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}, was dispatched as an envoy to investigate. Later, Tamichi led a military force to defeat the Silla troops. During the campaign, he captured and brought back the inhabitants of four settlements.
In the 55th year of Nintoku's reign, Tamichi was sent to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, but he was defeated and killed at the Ishinomito waterway. After his death, his attendant gave Tamichi’s keepsake, a jade he had wrapped around his hand, to his wife, who, stricken with grief, took her own life. Later, when the Emishi desecrated Tamichi’s grave, a giant serpent emerged and attacked them, causing many fatalities.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kudō Masaki|title=The Ancient History of the Emishi|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=2019|page=71}}</ref>
== Deification ==
Tamichi, who was sent north to suppress the Emishi, was later venerated as a deity of frontier development in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}} and {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北}}.<ref>https://toyohirajinja.or.jp/</ref> Major shrines dedicated to him include: * {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} * {{ill|Toyohira Shrine|ja|豊平神社}} * {{ill|Shinano Shrine|ja|信濃神社}}
== Banknote Depiction == [[File:Early one yen banknote front and reverse.jpg|thumb|Front and reverse of an early one-yen banknote issued by the {{ill|Second National Bank of Japan|ja|第二国立銀行}}. The right of the front features Tamichi.]] {{main|National Bank Notes of Japan#One Yen Note}} In 1873 ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 6), Tamichi was depicted on the one-yen convertible banknotes issued by the National Bank.<ref name="uemura">Uemura Takashi, ''Portraits on Banknotes in Modern History,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2015, pp. 66–67. ISBN 978-4-64-203845-4</ref>
Two theories exist regarding the design of the note: one suggests it depicts Kamitsukeno no Tamichi fending off a surprise attack by the Emishi, while the other posits it as depicting {{ill|Minamoto no Tametomo|ja|源為朝}} defending against Kudo Shigemitsu’s fleet. Notably, the figure is depicted with a disproportionately large head relative to the body, an unusual stylistic choice.<ref name="uemura"/>
== Legend == Legends about Tamichi are preserved at the {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} in {{ill|Hirakawa, Aomori|ja|平川市}}, {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}, and the {{ill|Toriya Shrine|ja|鳥屋神社}} in {{ill|Ishinomaki|ja|石巻市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}.<ref>http://saruka.webcrow.jp/</ref><ref>https://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010081</ref>
== Genealogy == According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' Tamichi was the son of {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}} and the younger brother of {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}.
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volume 11, Emperor Nintoku’s 55th Year.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|List of pre-Asuka period people|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}} * {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}} * {{ill|Tamichi Town|ja|田道町}}

{{Infobox noble | name = Kamitsukeno no Tamichi | image = Kamitsukenu-Tamichi.jpg | image_size = 220px | caption = {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}’s depiction of Tamichi in ''{{ill|Zenken Kojitsu|ja|前賢故実}}'' | era = {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Tamichi General | honorific_suffix = | deified_as = | grave = | rank = | allegiance = {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}} | clan = {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|下毛野氏|lt=Shimotsukeno clan}}, ancestor of the Tomi no Muraji | parents = {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}}<ref name="nakata">''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}''</ref> | siblings = {{ill|Takehase no Mikoto|ja|竹葉瀬命}}<ref name="nakata"/> | spouse = | notable_works = }}
'''Kamitsukeno no Tamichi''' (田道, birth date unknown - 55th year of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}’s reign) was a warrior of the Nintoku court. His clan and title are not described in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', but the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' refers to him as '''Tamichi no Kimi''' (田道公) with the kabane title of ''Kimi''.
== Overview ==
In the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} neglected its tribute obligations. Tamichi’s elder brother, {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}, was dispatched as an envoy to investigate. Later, Tamichi led a military force to defeat the Silla troops. During the campaign, he captured and brought back the inhabitants of four settlements.
In the 55th year of Nintoku's reign, Tamichi was sent to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, but he was defeated and killed at the Ishinomito waterway. After his death, his attendant gave Tamichi’s keepsake, a jade he had wrapped around his hand, to his wife, who, stricken with grief, took her own life. Later, when the Emishi desecrated Tamichi’s grave, a giant serpent emerged and attacked them, causing many fatalities.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kudō Masaki|title=The Ancient History of the Emishi|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=2019|page=71}}</ref>
== Deification ==
Tamichi, who was sent north to suppress the Emishi, was later venerated as a deity of frontier development in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}} and {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北}}.<ref>https://toyohirajinja.or.jp/</ref> Major shrines dedicated to him include: * {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} * {{ill|Toyohira Shrine|ja|豊平神社}} * {{ill|Shinano Shrine|ja|信濃神社}}
== Banknote Depiction == [[File:Early one yen banknote front and reverse.jpg|thumb|Front and reverse of an early one-yen banknote issued by the {{ill|Second National Bank of Japan|ja|第二国立銀行}}. The right of the front features Tamichi.]] {{main|National Bank Notes of Japan#One Yen Note}} In 1873 ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 6), Tamichi was depicted on the one-yen convertible banknotes issued by the National Bank.<ref name="uemura">Uemura Takashi, ''Portraits on Banknotes in Modern History,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2015, pp. 66–67. ISBN 978-4-64-203845-4</ref>
Two theories exist regarding the design of the note: one suggests it depicts Kamitsukeno no Tamichi fending off a surprise attack by the Emishi, while the other posits it as depicting {{ill|Minamoto no Tametomo|ja|源為朝}} defending against Kudo Shigemitsu’s fleet. Notably, the figure is depicted with a disproportionately large head relative to the body, an unusual stylistic choice.<ref name="uemura"/>
== Legend == Legends about Tamichi are preserved at the {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} in {{ill|Hirakawa, Aomori|ja|平川市}}, {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}, and the {{ill|Toriya Shrine|ja|鳥屋神社}} in {{ill|Ishinomaki|ja|石巻市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}.<ref>http://saruka.webcrow.jp/</ref><ref>https://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010081</ref>
== Genealogy == According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' Tamichi was the son of {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}} and the younger brother of {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}.
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volume 11, Emperor Nintoku’s 55th Year.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|List of pre-Asuka period people|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}} * {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}} * {{ill|Tamichi Town|ja|田道町}}

{{Infobox noble | name = Kamitsukeno no Tamichi | image = Kamitsukenu-Tamichi.jpg | image_size = 220px | caption = {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}’s depiction of Tamichi in ''{{ill|Zenken Kojitsu|ja|前賢故実}}'' | era = {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Tamichi General | honorific_suffix = | deified_as = | grave = | rank = | allegiance = {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}} | clan = {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|下毛野氏|lt=Shimotsukeno clan}}, ancestor of the Tomi no Muraji | parents = {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}}<ref name="nakata">''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}''</ref> | siblings = {{ill|Takehase no Mikoto|ja|竹葉瀬命}}<ref name="nakata"/> | spouse = | notable_works = }}
'''Kamitsukeno no Tamichi''' (田道, birth date unknown - 55th year of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}’s reign) was a warrior of the Nintoku court. His clan and title are not described in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', but the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' refers to him as '''Tamichi no Kimi''' (田道公) with the kabane title of ''Kimi''.
== Overview ==
In the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} neglected its tribute obligations. Tamichi’s elder brother, {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}, was dispatched as an envoy to investigate. Later, Tamichi led a military force to defeat the Silla troops. During the campaign, he captured and brought back the inhabitants of four settlements.
In the 55th year of Nintoku's reign, Tamichi was sent to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, but he was defeated and killed at the Ishinomito waterway. After his death, his attendant gave Tamichi’s keepsake, a jade he had wrapped around his hand, to his wife, who, stricken with grief, took her own life. Later, when the Emishi desecrated Tamichi’s grave, a giant serpent emerged and attacked them, causing many fatalities.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kudō Masaki|title=The Ancient History of the Emishi|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=2019|page=71}}</ref>
== Deification ==
Tamichi, who was sent north to suppress the Emishi, was later venerated as a deity of frontier development in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}} and {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北}}.<ref>https://toyohirajinja.or.jp/</ref> Major shrines dedicated to him include: * {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} * {{ill|Toyohira Shrine|ja|豊平神社}} * {{ill|Shinano Shrine|ja|信濃神社}}
== Banknote Depiction == [[File:Early one yen banknote front and reverse.jpg|thumb|Front and reverse of an early one-yen banknote issued by the {{ill|Second National Bank of Japan|ja|第二国立銀行}}. The right of the front features Tamichi.]] {{main|National Bank Notes of Japan#One Yen Note}} In 1873 ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 6), Tamichi was depicted on the one-yen convertible banknotes issued by the National Bank.<ref name="uemura">Uemura Takashi, ''Portraits on Banknotes in Modern History,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2015, pp. 66–67. ISBN 978-4-64-203845-4</ref>
Two theories exist regarding the design of the note: one suggests it depicts Kamitsukeno no Tamichi fending off a surprise attack by the Emishi, while the other posits it as depicting {{ill|Minamoto no Tametomo|ja|源為朝}} defending against Kudo Shigemitsu’s fleet. Notably, the figure is depicted with a disproportionately large head relative to the body, an unusual stylistic choice.<ref name="uemura"/>
== Legend == Legends about Tamichi are preserved at the {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} in {{ill|Hirakawa, Aomori|ja|平川市}}, {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}, and the {{ill|Toriya Shrine|ja|鳥屋神社}} in {{ill|Ishinomaki|ja|石巻市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}.<ref>http://saruka.webcrow.jp/</ref><ref>https://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010081</ref>
== Genealogy == According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' Tamichi was the son of {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}} and the younger brother of {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}.
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volume 11, Emperor Nintoku’s 55th Year.

{{Infobox noble | name = Kamitsukeno no Tamichi | image = Kamitsukenu-Tamichi.jpg | image_size = 220px | caption = {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}’s depiction of Tamichi in ''{{ill|Zenken Kojitsu|ja|前賢故実}}'' | era = {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Tamichi General | honorific_suffix = | deified_as = | grave = | rank = | allegiance = {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}} | clan = {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|下毛野氏|lt=Shimotsukeno clan}}, ancestor of the Tomi no Muraji | parents = {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}}<ref name="nakata">''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}''</ref> | siblings = {{ill|Takehase no Mikoto|ja|竹葉瀬命}}<ref name="nakata"/> | spouse = | notable_works = }}
'''Kamitsukeno no Tamichi''' (田道, birth date unknown - 55th year of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}’s reign) was a warrior of the Nintoku court. His clan and title are not described in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', but the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' refers to him as '''Tamichi no Kimi''' (田道公) with the kabane title of ''Kimi''.
== Overview ==
In the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} neglected its tribute obligations. Tamichi’s elder brother, {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}, was dispatched as an envoy to investigate. Later, Tamichi led a military force to defeat the Silla troops. During the campaign, he captured and brought back the inhabitants of four settlements.
In the 55th year of Nintoku's reign, Tamichi was sent to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, but he was defeated and killed at the Ishinomito waterway. After his death, his attendant gave Tamichi’s keepsake, a jade he had wrapped around his hand, to his wife, who, stricken with grief, took her own life. Later, when the Emishi desecrated Tamichi’s grave, a giant serpent emerged and attacked them, causing many fatalities.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kudō Masaki|title=The Ancient History of the Emishi|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=2019|page=71}}</ref>
== Deification ==
Tamichi, who was sent north to suppress the Emishi, was later venerated as a deity of frontier development in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}} and {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北}}.<ref>https://toyohirajinja.or.jp/</ref> Major shrines dedicated to him include: * {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} * {{ill|Toyohira Shrine|ja|豊平神社}} * {{ill|Shinano Shrine|ja|信濃神社}}
== Banknote Depiction == [[File:Early one yen banknote front and reverse.jpg|thumb|Front and reverse of an early one-yen banknote issued by the {{ill|Second National Bank of Japan|ja|第二国立銀行}}. The right of the front features Tamichi.]] {{main|National Bank Notes of Japan#One Yen Note}} In 1873 ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 6), Tamichi was depicted on the one-yen convertible banknotes issued by the National Bank.<ref name="uemura">Uemura Takashi, ''Portraits on Banknotes in Modern History,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2015, pp. 66–67. ISBN 978-4-64-203845-4</ref>
Two theories exist regarding the design of the note: one suggests it depicts Kamitsukeno no Tamichi fending off a surprise attack by the Emishi, while the other posits it as depicting {{ill|Minamoto no Tametomo|ja|源為朝}} defending against Kudo Shigemitsu’s fleet. Notably, the figure is depicted with a disproportionately large head relative to the body, an unusual stylistic choice.<ref name="uemura"/>
== Legend == Legends about Tamichi are preserved at the {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} in {{ill|Hirakawa, Aomori|ja|平川市}}, {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}, and the {{ill|Toriya Shrine|ja|鳥屋神社}} in {{ill|Ishinomaki|ja|石巻市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}.<ref>http://saruka.webcrow.jp/</ref><ref>https://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010081</ref>
== Genealogy == According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' Tamichi was the son of {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}} and the younger brother of {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}.
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volume 11, Emperor Nintoku’s 55th Year.
== Notes == {{reflist}}

{{Infobox noble | name = Kamitsukeno no Tamichi | image = Kamitsukenu-Tamichi.jpg | image_size = 220px | caption = {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}’s depiction of Tamichi in ''{{ill|Zenken Kojitsu|ja|前賢故実}}'' | era = {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Tamichi General | honorific_suffix = | deified_as = | grave = | rank = | allegiance = {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}} | clan = {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|下毛野氏|lt=Shimotsukeno clan}}, ancestor of the Tomi no Muraji | parents = {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}}<ref name="nakata">''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}''</ref> | siblings = {{ill|Takehase no Mikoto|ja|竹葉瀬命}}<ref name="nakata"/> | spouse = | notable_works = }}
'''Kamitsukeno no Tamichi''' (田道, birth date unknown - 55th year of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}’s reign) was a warrior of the Nintoku court. His clan and title are not described in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', but the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' refers to him as '''Tamichi no Kimi''' (田道公) with the kabane title of ''Kimi''.
== Overview ==
In the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} neglected its tribute obligations. Tamichi’s elder brother, {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}, was dispatched as an envoy to investigate. Later, Tamichi led a military force to defeat the Silla troops. During the campaign, he captured and brought back the inhabitants of four settlements.
In the 55th year of Nintoku's reign, Tamichi was sent to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, but he was defeated and killed at the Ishinomito waterway. After his death, his attendant gave Tamichi’s keepsake, a jade he had wrapped around his hand, to his wife, who, stricken with grief, took her own life. Later, when the Emishi desecrated Tamichi’s grave, a giant serpent emerged and attacked them, causing many fatalities.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kudō Masaki|title=The Ancient History of the Emishi|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=2019|page=71}}</ref>
== Deification ==
Tamichi, who was sent north to suppress the Emishi, was later venerated as a deity of frontier development in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}} and {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北}}.<ref>https://toyohirajinja.or.jp/</ref> Major shrines dedicated to him include: * {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} * {{ill|Toyohira Shrine|ja|豊平神社}} * {{ill|Shinano Shrine|ja|信濃神社}}
== Banknote Depiction == [[File:Early one yen banknote front and reverse.jpg|thumb|Front and reverse of an early one-yen banknote issued by the {{ill|Second National Bank of Japan|ja|第二国立銀行}}. The right of the front features Tamichi.]] {{main|National Bank Notes of Japan#One Yen Note}} In 1873 ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 6), Tamichi was depicted on the one-yen convertible banknotes issued by the National Bank.<ref name="uemura">Uemura Takashi, ''Portraits on Banknotes in Modern History,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2015, pp. 66–67. ISBN 978-4-64-203845-4</ref>
Two theories exist regarding the design of the note: one suggests it depicts Kamitsukeno no Tamichi fending off a surprise attack by the Emishi, while the other posits it as depicting {{ill|Minamoto no Tametomo|ja|源為朝}} defending against Kudo Shigemitsu’s fleet. Notably, the figure is depicted with a disproportionately large head relative to the body, an unusual stylistic choice.<ref name="uemura"/>
== Legend == Legends about Tamichi are preserved at the {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} in {{ill|Hirakawa, Aomori|ja|平川市}}, {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}, and the {{ill|Toriya Shrine|ja|鳥屋神社}} in {{ill|Ishinomaki|ja|石巻市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}.<ref>http://saruka.webcrow.jp/</ref><ref>https://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010081</ref>
== Genealogy == According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' Tamichi was the son of {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}} and the younger brother of {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}.
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volume 11, Emperor Nintoku’s 55th Year.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|List of pre-Asuka period people|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}} * {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}} * {{ill|Tamichi Town|ja|田道町}}

'''Kamitsukeno no Tamichi''' (田道, birth date unknown - 55th year of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}’s reign) was a warrior of the Nintoku court. His clan and title are not described in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', but the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' refers to him as '''Tamichi no Kimi''' (田道公) with the kabane title of ''Kimi''.

Later, Tamichi led a military force to defeat the Silla troops. During the campaign, he captured and brought back the inhabitants of four settlements.
In the 55th year of Nintoku's reign, Tamichi was sent to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, but he was defeated and killed at the Ishinomito waterway. After his death, his attendant gave Tamichi’s keepsake, a jade he had wrapped around his hand, to his wife, who, stricken with grief, took her own life. Later, when the Emishi desecrated Tamichi’s grave, a giant serpent emerged and attacked them, causing many fatalities.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kudō Masaki|title=The Ancient History of the Emishi|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=2019|page=71}}</ref>

The right of the front features Tamichi.]] {{main|National Bank Notes of Japan#One Yen Note}} In 1873 ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 6), Tamichi was depicted on the one-yen convertible banknotes issued by the National Bank.<ref name="uemura">Uemura Takashi, ''Portraits on Banknotes in Modern History,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2015, pp. 66–67. ISBN 978-4-64-203845-4</ref>
Two theories exist regarding the design of the note: one suggests it depicts Kamitsukeno no Tamichi fending off a surprise attack by the Emishi, while the other posits it as depicting {{ill|Minamoto no Tametomo|ja|源為朝}} defending against Kudo Shigemitsu’s fleet. Notably, the figure is depicted with a disproportionately large head relative to the body, an unusual stylistic choice.<ref name="uemura"/>

revision.text:
{{Infobox noble | name = Kamitsukeno no Tamichi | image = Kamitsukenu-Tamichi.jpg | image_size = 220px | caption = {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}’s depiction of Tamichi in ''{{ill|Zenken Kojitsu|ja|前賢故実}}'' | era = {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Tamichi General | honorific_suffix = | deified_as = | grave = | rank = | allegiance = {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}} | clan = {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|下毛野氏|lt=Shimotsukeno clan}}, ancestor of the Tomi no Muraji | parents = {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}}<ref name="nakata">''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}''</ref> | siblings = {{ill|Takehase no Mikoto|ja|竹葉瀬命}}<ref name="nakata"/> | spouse = | notable_works = }}
'''Kamitsukeno no Tamichi''' (田道, birth date unknown - 55th year of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}’s reign) was a warrior of the Nintoku court. His clan and title are not described in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', but the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' refers to him as '''Tamichi no Kimi''' (田道公) with the kabane title of ''Kimi''.
== Overview ==
In the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} neglected its tribute obligations. Tamichi’s elder brother, {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}, was dispatched as an envoy to investigate. Later, Tamichi led a military force to defeat the Silla troops. During the campaign, he captured and brought back the inhabitants of four settlements.
In the 55th year of Nintoku's reign, Tamichi was sent to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, but he was defeated and killed at the Ishinomito waterway. After his death, his attendant gave Tamichi’s keepsake, a jade he had wrapped around his hand, to his wife, who, stricken with grief, took her own life. Later, when the Emishi desecrated Tamichi’s grave, a giant serpent emerged and attacked them, causing many fatalities.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kudō Masaki|title=The Ancient History of the Emishi|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=2019|page=71}}</ref>
== Deification ==
Tamichi, who was sent north to suppress the Emishi, was later venerated as a deity of frontier development in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}} and {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北}}.<ref>https://toyohirajinja.or.jp/</ref> Major shrines dedicated to him include: * {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} * {{ill|Toyohira Shrine|ja|豊平神社}} * {{ill|Shinano Shrine|ja|信濃神社}}
== Banknote Depiction == [[File:Early one yen banknote front and reverse.jpg|thumb|Front and reverse of an early one-yen banknote issued by the {{ill|Second National Bank of Japan|ja|第二国立銀行}}. The right of the front features Tamichi.]] {{main|National Bank Notes of Japan#One Yen Note}} In 1873 ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 6), Tamichi was depicted on the one-yen convertible banknotes issued by the National Bank.<ref name="uemura">Uemura Takashi, ''Portraits on Banknotes in Modern History,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2015, pp. 66–67. ISBN 978-4-64-203845-4</ref>
Two theories exist regarding the design of the note: one suggests it depicts Kamitsukeno no Tamichi fending off a surprise attack by the Emishi, while the other posits it as depicting {{ill|Minamoto no Tametomo|ja|源為朝}} defending against Kudo Shigemitsu’s fleet. Notably, the figure is depicted with a disproportionately large head relative to the body, an unusual stylistic choice.<ref name="uemura"/>
== Legend == Legends about Tamichi are preserved at the {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} in {{ill|Hirakawa, Aomori|ja|平川市}}, {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}, and the {{ill|Toriya Shrine|ja|鳥屋神社}} in {{ill|Ishinomaki|ja|石巻市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}.<ref>http://saruka.webcrow.jp/</ref><ref>https://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010081</ref>
== Genealogy == According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' Tamichi was the son of {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}} and the younger brother of {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}.
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volume 11, Emperor Nintoku’s 55th Year.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|List of pre-Asuka period people|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}} * {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}} * {{ill|Tamichi Town|ja|田道町}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox noble | name = Kamitsukeno no Tamichi | image = Kamitsukenu-Tamichi.jpg | image_size = 220px | caption = {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}’s depiction of Tamichi in ''{{ill|Zenken Kojitsu|ja|前賢故実}}'' | era = {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Tamichi General | honorific_suffix = | deified_as = | grave = | rank = | allegiance = {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}} | clan = {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|下毛野氏|lt=Shimotsukeno clan}}, ancestor of the Tomi no Muraji | parents = {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}}<ref name="nakata">''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}''</ref> | siblings = {{ill|Takehase no Mikoto|ja|竹葉瀬命}}<ref name="nakata"/> | spouse = | notable_works = }}
'''Kamitsukeno no Tamichi''' (田道, birth date unknown - 55th year of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}’s reign) was a warrior of the Nintoku court. His clan and title are not described in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', but the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' refers to him as '''Tamichi no Kimi''' (田道公) with the kabane title of ''Kimi''.
== Overview ==
In the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} neglected its tribute obligations. Tamichi’s elder brother, {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}, was dispatched as an envoy to investigate. Later, Tamichi led a military force to defeat the Silla troops. During the campaign, he captured and brought back the inhabitants of four settlements.
In the 55th year of Nintoku's reign, Tamichi was sent to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, but he was defeated and killed at the Ishinomito waterway. After his death, his attendant gave Tamichi’s keepsake, a jade he had wrapped around his hand, to his wife, who, stricken with grief, took her own life. Later, when the Emishi desecrated Tamichi’s grave, a giant serpent emerged and attacked them, causing many fatalities.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kudō Masaki|title=The Ancient History of the Emishi|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=2019|page=71}}</ref>
== Deification ==
Tamichi, who was sent north to suppress the Emishi, was later venerated as a deity of frontier development in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}} and {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北}}.<ref>https://toyohirajinja.or.jp/</ref> Major shrines dedicated to him include: * {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} * {{ill|Toyohira Shrine|ja|豊平神社}} * {{ill|Shinano Shrine|ja|信濃神社}}
== Banknote Depiction == [[File:Early one yen banknote front and reverse.jpg|thumb|Front and reverse of an early one-yen banknote issued by the {{ill|Second National Bank of Japan|ja|第二国立銀行}}. The right of the front features Tamichi.]] {{main|National Bank Notes of Japan#One Yen Note}} In 1873 ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 6), Tamichi was depicted on the one-yen convertible banknotes issued by the National Bank.<ref name="uemura">Uemura Takashi, ''Portraits on Banknotes in Modern History,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2015, pp. 66–67. ISBN 978-4-64-203845-4</ref>
Two theories exist regarding the design of the note: one suggests it depicts Kamitsukeno no Tamichi fending off a surprise attack by the Emishi, while the other posits it as depicting {{ill|Minamoto no Tametomo|ja|源為朝}} defending against Kudo Shigemitsu’s fleet. Notably, the figure is depicted with a disproportionately large head relative to the body, an unusual stylistic choice.<ref name="uemura"/>
== Legend == Legends about Tamichi are preserved at the {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} in {{ill|Hirakawa, Aomori|ja|平川市}}, {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}, and the {{ill|Toriya Shrine|ja|鳥屋神社}} in {{ill|Ishinomaki|ja|石巻市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}.<ref>http://saruka.webcrow.jp/</ref><ref>https://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010081</ref>
== Genealogy == According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' Tamichi was the son of {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}} and the younger brother of {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}.
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volume 11, Emperor Nintoku’s 55th Year.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|List of pre-Asuka period people|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}} * {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}} * {{ill|Tamichi Town|ja|田道町}}

{{Infobox noble | name = Kamitsukeno no Tamichi | image = Kamitsukenu-Tamichi.jpg | image_size = 220px | caption = {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}’s depiction of Tamichi in ''{{ill|Zenken Kojitsu|ja|前賢故実}}'' | era = {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Tamichi General | honorific_suffix = | deified_as = | grave = | rank = | allegiance = {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}} | clan = {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|下毛野氏|1=Shimotsukeno clan|lt=Shimotsukeno clan}}, ancestor of the Tomi no Muraji | parents = {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}}<ref name="nakata">''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}''</ref> | siblings = {{ill|Takehase no Mikoto|ja|竹葉瀬命}}<ref name="nakata"/> | spouse = | notable_works = }}

His clan and title are not described in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', but the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' refers to him as '''Tamichi no Kimi''' (田道公) with the kabane title of ''Kimi''.

ISBN 978-4-64-203845-4</ref>

{{Infobox noble | name = Kamitsukeno no Tamichi | image = Kamitsukenu-Tamichi.jpg | image_size = 220px | caption = {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}’s depiction of Tamichi in ''{{ill|Zenken Kojitsu|ja|前賢故実}}'' | era = {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Tamichi General | honorific_suffix = | deified_as = | grave = | rank = | allegiance = {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}} | clan = {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|下毛野氏|1=Shimotsukeno clan|lt=Shimotsukeno clan}}, ancestor of the Tomi no Muraji | parents = {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}}<ref name="nakata">''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}''</ref> | siblings = {{ill|Takehase no Mikoto|ja|竹葉瀬命}}<ref name="nakata"/> | spouse = | notable_works = }}
'''Kamitsukeno no Tamichi''' (田道, birth date unknown - 55th year of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}’s reign) was a warrior of the Nintoku court. His clan and title are not described in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', but the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' refers to him as '''Tamichi no Kimi''' (田道公) with the kabane title of ''Kimi''.
== Overview ==
In the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} neglected its tribute obligations. Tamichi’s elder brother, {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}, was dispatched as an envoy to investigate. Later, Tamichi led a military force to defeat the Silla troops. During the campaign, he captured and brought back the inhabitants of four settlements.
In the 55th year of Nintoku's reign, Tamichi was sent to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, but he was defeated and killed at the Ishinomito waterway. After his death, his attendant gave Tamichi’s keepsake, a jade he had wrapped around his hand, to his wife, who, stricken with grief, took her own life. Later, when the Emishi desecrated Tamichi’s grave, a giant serpent emerged and attacked them, causing many fatalities.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kudō Masaki|title=The Ancient History of the Emishi|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=2019|page=71}}</ref>
== Deification ==
Tamichi, who was sent north to suppress the Emishi, was later venerated as a deity of frontier development in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}} and {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北}}.<ref>https://toyohirajinja.or.jp/</ref> Major shrines dedicated to him include: * {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} * {{ill|Toyohira Shrine|ja|豊平神社}} * {{ill|Shinano Shrine|ja|信濃神社}}
== Banknote Depiction == [[File:Early one yen banknote front and reverse.jpg|thumb|Front and reverse of an early one-yen banknote issued by the {{ill|Second National Bank of Japan|ja|第二国立銀行}}. The right of the front features Tamichi.]] {{main|National Bank Notes of Japan#One Yen Note}} In 1873 ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 6), Tamichi was depicted on the one-yen convertible banknotes issued by the National Bank.<ref name="uemura">Uemura Takashi, ''Portraits on Banknotes in Modern History,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2015, pp. 66–67. ISBN 978-4-64-203845-4</ref>
Two theories exist regarding the design of the note: one suggests it depicts Kamitsukeno no Tamichi fending off a surprise attack by the Emishi, while the other posits it as depicting {{ill|Minamoto no Tametomo|ja|源為朝}} defending against Kudo Shigemitsu’s fleet. Notably, the figure is depicted with a disproportionately large head relative to the body, an unusual stylistic choice.<ref name="uemura"/>
== Legend == Legends about Tamichi are preserved at the {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} in {{ill|Hirakawa, Aomori|ja|平川市}}, {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}, and the {{ill|Toriya Shrine|ja|鳥屋神社}} in {{ill|Ishinomaki|ja|石巻市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}.<ref>http://saruka.webcrow.jp/</ref><ref>https://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010081</ref>
== Genealogy == According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' Tamichi was the son of {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}} and the younger brother of {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}.
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volume 11, Emperor Nintoku’s 55th Year.

revision.text:
{{Infobox noble | name = Kamitsukeno no Tamichi | image = Kamitsukenu-Tamichi.jpg | image_size = 220px | caption = {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}’s depiction of Tamichi in ''{{ill|Zenken Kojitsu|ja|前賢故実}}'' | era = {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Tamichi General | honorific_suffix = | deified_as = | grave = | rank = | allegiance = {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}} | clan = {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|下毛野氏|1=Shimotsukeno clan|lt=Shimotsukeno clan}}, ancestor of the Tomi no Muraji | parents = {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}}<ref name="nakata">''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}''</ref> | siblings = {{ill|Takehase no Mikoto|ja|竹葉瀬命}}<ref name="nakata"/> | spouse = | notable_works = }}
'''Kamitsukeno no Tamichi''' (田道, birth date unknown - 55th year of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}’s reign) was a warrior of the Nintoku court. His clan and title are not described in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', but the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' refers to him as '''Tamichi no Kimi''' (田道公) with the kabane title of ''Kimi''.
== Overview ==
In the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} neglected its tribute obligations. Tamichi’s elder brother, {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}, was dispatched as an envoy to investigate. Later, Tamichi led a military force to defeat the Silla troops. During the campaign, he captured and brought back the inhabitants of four settlements.
In the 55th year of Nintoku's reign, Tamichi was sent to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, but he was defeated and killed at the Ishinomito waterway. After his death, his attendant gave Tamichi’s keepsake, a jade he had wrapped around his hand, to his wife, who, stricken with grief, took her own life. Later, when the Emishi desecrated Tamichi’s grave, a giant serpent emerged and attacked them, causing many fatalities.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kudō Masaki|title=The Ancient History of the Emishi|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=2019|page=71}}</ref>
== Deification ==
Tamichi, who was sent north to suppress the Emishi, was later venerated as a deity of frontier development in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}} and {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北}}.<ref>https://toyohirajinja.or.jp/</ref> Major shrines dedicated to him include: * {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} * {{ill|Toyohira Shrine|ja|豊平神社}} * {{ill|Shinano Shrine|ja|信濃神社}}
== Banknote Depiction == [[File:Early one yen banknote front and reverse.jpg|thumb|Front and reverse of an early one-yen banknote issued by the {{ill|Second National Bank of Japan|ja|第二国立銀行}}. The right of the front features Tamichi.]] {{main|National Bank Notes of Japan#One Yen Note}} In 1873 ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 6), Tamichi was depicted on the one-yen convertible banknotes issued by the National Bank.<ref name="uemura">Uemura Takashi, ''Portraits on Banknotes in Modern History,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2015, pp. 66–67. ISBN 978-4-64-203845-4</ref>
Two theories exist regarding the design of the note: one suggests it depicts Kamitsukeno no Tamichi fending off a surprise attack by the Emishi, while the other posits it as depicting {{ill|Minamoto no Tametomo|ja|源為朝}} defending against Kudo Shigemitsu’s fleet. Notably, the figure is depicted with a disproportionately large head relative to the body, an unusual stylistic choice.<ref name="uemura"/>
== Legend == Legends about Tamichi are preserved at the {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} in {{ill|Hirakawa, Aomori|ja|平川市}}, {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}, and the {{ill|Toriya Shrine|ja|鳥屋神社}} in {{ill|Ishinomaki|ja|石巻市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}.<ref>http://saruka.webcrow.jp/</ref><ref>https://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010081</ref>
== Genealogy == According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' Tamichi was the son of {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}} and the younger brother of {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}.
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volume 11, Emperor Nintoku’s 55th Year.
== Notes == {{reflist}}== See Also == * {{ill|List of pre-Asuka period people|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}} * {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}} * {{ill|Tamichi Town|ja|田道町}}

{{Infobox noble | name = Kamitsukeno no Tamichi | image = Kamitsukenu-Tamichi.jpg | image_size = 220px | caption = {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}’s depiction of Tamichi in ''{{ill|Zenken Kojitsu|ja|前賢故実}}'' | era = {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Tamichi General | honorific_suffix = | deified_as = | grave = | rank = | allegiance = {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}} | clan = {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|下毛野氏|1=Shimotsukeno clan|lt=Shimotsukeno clan}}, ancestor of the Tomi no Muraji | parents = {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}}<ref name="nakata">''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}''</ref> | siblings = {{ill|Takehase no Mikoto|ja|竹葉瀬命}}<ref name="nakata"/> | spouse = | notable_works = }}
'''Kamitsukeno no Tamichi''' (田道, birth date unknown - 55th year of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}’s reign) was a warrior of the Nintoku court. His clan and title are not described in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', but the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' refers to him as '''Tamichi no Kimi''' (田道公) with the kabane title of ''Kimi''.
== Overview ==
In the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} neglected its tribute obligations. Tamichi’s elder brother, {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}, was dispatched as an envoy to investigate. Later, Tamichi led a military force to defeat the Silla troops. During the campaign, he captured and brought back the inhabitants of four settlements.
In the 55th year of Nintoku's reign, Tamichi was sent to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, but he was defeated and killed at the Ishinomito waterway. After his death, his attendant gave Tamichi’s keepsake, a jade he had wrapped around his hand, to his wife, who, stricken with grief, took her own life. Later, when the Emishi desecrated Tamichi’s grave, a giant serpent emerged and attacked them, causing many fatalities.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kudō Masaki|title=The Ancient History of the Emishi|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=2019|page=71}}</ref>
== Deification ==
Tamichi, who was sent north to suppress the Emishi, was later venerated as a deity of frontier development in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}} and {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北}}.<ref>https://toyohirajinja.or.jp/</ref> Major shrines dedicated to him include: * {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} * {{ill|Toyohira Shrine|ja|豊平神社}} * {{ill|Shinano Shrine|ja|信濃神社}}
== Banknote Depiction == [[File:Early one yen banknote front and reverse.jpg|thumb|Front and reverse of an early one-yen banknote issued by the {{ill|Second National Bank of Japan|ja|第二国立銀行}}. The right of the front features Tamichi.]] {{main|National Bank Notes of Japan#One Yen Note}} In 1873 ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 6), Tamichi was depicted on the one-yen convertible banknotes issued by the National Bank.<ref name="uemura">Uemura Takashi, ''Portraits on Banknotes in Modern History,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2015, pp. 66–67. ISBN 978-4-64-203845-4</ref>
Two theories exist regarding the design of the note: one suggests it depicts Kamitsukeno no Tamichi fending off a surprise attack by the Emishi, while the other posits it as depicting {{ill|Minamoto no Tametomo|ja|源為朝}} defending against Kudo Shigemitsu’s fleet. Notably, the figure is depicted with a disproportionately large head relative to the body, an unusual stylistic choice.<ref name="uemura"/>
== Legend == Legends about Tamichi are preserved at the {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} in {{ill|Hirakawa, Aomori|ja|平川市}}, {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}, and the {{ill|Toriya Shrine|ja|鳥屋神社}} in {{ill|Ishinomaki|ja|石巻市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}.<ref>http://saruka.webcrow.jp/</ref><ref>https://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010081</ref>
== Genealogy == According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' Tamichi was the son of {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}} and the younger brother of {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}.

revision.text:
{{Infobox noble | name = Kamitsukeno no Tamichi | image = Kamitsukenu-Tamichi.jpg | image_size = 220px | caption = {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}’s depiction of Tamichi in ''{{ill|Zenken Kojitsu|ja|前賢故実}}'' | era = {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Tamichi General | honorific_suffix = | deified_as = | grave = | rank = | allegiance = {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}} | clan = {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|下毛野氏|1=Shimotsukeno clan|lt=Shimotsukeno clan}}, ancestor of the Tomi no Muraji | parents = {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}}<ref name="nakata">''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}''</ref> | siblings = {{ill|Takehase no Mikoto|ja|竹葉瀬命}}<ref name="nakata"/> | spouse = | notable_works = }}
'''Kamitsukeno no Tamichi''' (田道, birth date unknown - 55th year of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}’s reign) was a warrior of the Nintoku court. His clan and title are not described in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', but the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' refers to him as '''Tamichi no Kimi''' (田道公) with the kabane title of ''Kimi''.
== Overview ==
In the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} neglected its tribute obligations. Tamichi’s elder brother, {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}, was dispatched as an envoy to investigate. Later, Tamichi led a military force to defeat the Silla troops. During the campaign, he captured and brought back the inhabitants of four settlements.
In the 55th year of Nintoku's reign, Tamichi was sent to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, but he was defeated and killed at the Ishinomito waterway. After his death, his attendant gave Tamichi’s keepsake, a jade he had wrapped around his hand, to his wife, who, stricken with grief, took her own life. Later, when the Emishi desecrated Tamichi’s grave, a giant serpent emerged and attacked them, causing many fatalities.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kudō Masaki|title=The Ancient History of the Emishi|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=2019|page=71}}</ref>
== Deification ==
Tamichi, who was sent north to suppress the Emishi, was later venerated as a deity of frontier development in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}} and {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北}}.<ref>https://toyohirajinja.or.jp/</ref> Major shrines dedicated to him include: * {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} * {{ill|Toyohira Shrine|ja|豊平神社}} * {{ill|Shinano Shrine|ja|信濃神社}}
== Banknote Depiction == [[File:Early one yen banknote front and reverse.jpg|thumb|Front and reverse of an early one-yen banknote issued by the {{ill|Second National Bank of Japan|ja|第二国立銀行}}. The right of the front features Tamichi.]] {{main|National Bank Notes of Japan#One Yen Note}} In 1873 ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 6), Tamichi was depicted on the one-yen convertible banknotes issued by the National Bank.<ref name="uemura">Uemura Takashi, ''Portraits on Banknotes in Modern History,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2015, pp. 66–67. ISBN 978-4-64-203845-4</ref>
Two theories exist regarding the design of the note: one suggests it depicts Kamitsukeno no Tamichi fending off a surprise attack by the Emishi, while the other posits it as depicting {{ill|Minamoto no Tametomo|ja|源為朝}} defending against Kudo Shigemitsu’s fleet. Notably, the figure is depicted with a disproportionately large head relative to the body, an unusual stylistic choice.<ref name="uemura"/>
== Legend == Legends about Tamichi are preserved at the {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} in {{ill|Hirakawa, Aomori|ja|平川市}}, {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}, and the {{ill|Toriya Shrine|ja|鳥屋神社}} in {{ill|Ishinomaki|ja|石巻市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}.<ref>http://saruka.webcrow.jp/</ref><ref>https://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010081</ref>
== Genealogy == According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' Tamichi was the son of {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}} and the younger brother of {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}.
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volume 11, Emperor Nintoku’s 55th Year.
== Notes == {{reflist}} == See Also! == * {{ill|List of pre-Asuka period people|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}} * {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}} * {{ill|Tamichi Town|ja|田道町}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox noble | name = Kamitsukeno no Tamichi | image = Kamitsukenu-Tamichi.jpg | image_size = 220px | caption = {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}’s depiction of Tamichi in ''{{ill|Zenken Kojitsu|ja|前賢故実}}'' | era = {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Tamichi General | honorific_suffix = | deified_as = | grave = | rank = | allegiance = {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}} | clan = {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|下毛野氏|1=Shimotsukeno clan|lt=Shimotsukeno clan}}, ancestor of the Tomi no Muraji | parents = {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}}<ref name="nakata">''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}''</ref> | siblings = {{ill|Takehase no Mikoto|ja|竹葉瀬命}}<ref name="nakata"/> | spouse = | notable_works = }}
'''Kamitsukeno no Tamichi''' (田道, birth date unknown - 55th year of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}’s reign) was a warrior of the Nintoku court. His clan and title are not described in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', but the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' refers to him as '''Tamichi no Kimi''' (田道公) with the kabane title of ''Kimi''.
== Overview ==
In the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} neglected its tribute obligations. Tamichi’s elder brother, {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}, was dispatched as an envoy to investigate. Later, Tamichi led a military force to defeat the Silla troops. During the campaign, he captured and brought back the inhabitants of four settlements.
In the 55th year of Nintoku's reign, Tamichi was sent to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, but he was defeated and killed at the Ishinomito waterway. After his death, his attendant gave Tamichi’s keepsake, a jade he had wrapped around his hand, to his wife, who, stricken with grief, took her own life. Later, when the Emishi desecrated Tamichi’s grave, a giant serpent emerged and attacked them, causing many fatalities.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kudō Masaki|title=The Ancient History of the Emishi|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=2019|page=71}}</ref>
== Deification ==
Tamichi, who was sent north to suppress the Emishi, was later venerated as a deity of frontier development in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}} and {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北}}.<ref>https://toyohirajinja.or.jp/</ref> Major shrines dedicated to him include: * {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} * {{ill|Toyohira Shrine|ja|豊平神社}} * {{ill|Shinano Shrine|ja|信濃神社}}
== Banknote Depiction == [[File:Early one yen banknote front and reverse.jpg|thumb|Front and reverse of an early one-yen banknote issued by the {{ill|Second National Bank of Japan|ja|第二国立銀行}}. The right of the front features Tamichi.]] {{main|National Bank Notes of Japan#One Yen Note}} In 1873 ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 6), Tamichi was depicted on the one-yen convertible banknotes issued by the National Bank.<ref name="uemura">Uemura Takashi, ''Portraits on Banknotes in Modern History,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2015, pp. 66–67. ISBN 978-4-64-203845-4</ref>
Two theories exist regarding the design of the note: one suggests it depicts Kamitsukeno no Tamichi fending off a surprise attack by the Emishi, while the other posits it as depicting {{ill|Minamoto no Tametomo|ja|源為朝}} defending against Kudo Shigemitsu’s fleet. Notably, the figure is depicted with a disproportionately large head relative to the body, an unusual stylistic choice.<ref name="uemura"/>
== Legend == Legends about Tamichi are preserved at the {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} in {{ill|Hirakawa, Aomori|ja|平川市}}, {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}, and the {{ill|Toriya Shrine|ja|鳥屋神社}} in {{ill|Ishinomaki|ja|石巻市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}.<ref>http://saruka.webcrow.jp/</ref><ref>https://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010081</ref>
== Genealogy == According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' Tamichi was the son of {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}} and the younger brother of {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}.
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volume 11, Emperor Nintoku’s 55th Year.
== Notes == {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of pre-Asuka period people|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}} * {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}} * {{ill|Tamichi Town|ja|田道町}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox noble | name = Kamitsukeno no Tamichi | image = Kamitsukenu-Tamichi.jpg | image_size = 220px | caption = {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}’s depiction of Tamichi in ''{{ill|Zenken Kojitsu|ja|前賢故実}}'' | era = {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Tamichi General | honorific_suffix = | deified_as = | grave = | rank = | allegiance = {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}} | clan = {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|下毛野氏|1=Shimotsukeno clan|lt=Shimotsukeno clan}}, ancestor of the Tomi no Muraji | parents = {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}}<ref name="nakata">''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}''</ref> | siblings = {{ill|Takehase no Mikoto|ja|竹葉瀬命}}<ref name="nakata"/> | spouse = | notable_works = }}
'''Kamitsukeno no Tamichi''' (田道, birth date unknown - 55th year of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}’s reign) was a warrior of the Nintoku court. His clan and title are not described in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', but the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' refers to him as '''Tamichi no Kimi''' (田道公) with the kabane title of ''Kimi''.
== Overview ==
In the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} neglected its tribute obligations. Tamichi’s elder brother, {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}, was dispatched as an envoy to investigate. Later, Tamichi led a military force to defeat the Silla troops. During the campaign, he captured and brought back the inhabitants of four settlements.
In the 55th year of Nintoku's reign, Tamichi was sent to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, but he was defeated and killed at the Ishinomito waterway. After his death, his attendant gave Tamichi’s keepsake, a jade he had wrapped around his hand, to his wife, who, stricken with grief, took her own life. Later, when the Emishi desecrated Tamichi’s grave, a giant serpent emerged and attacked them, causing many fatalities.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kudō Masaki|title=The Ancient History of the Emishi|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=2019|page=71}}</ref>
== Deification ==
Tamichi, who was sent north to suppress the Emishi, was later venerated as a deity of frontier development in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}} and {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北}}.<ref>https://toyohirajinja.or.jp/</ref> Major shrines dedicated to him include: * {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} * {{ill|Toyohira Shrine|ja|豊平神社}} * {{ill|Shinano Shrine|ja|信濃神社}}
== Banknote Depiction == [[File:Early one yen banknote front and reverse.jpg|thumb|Front and reverse of an early one-yen banknote issued by the {{ill|Second National Bank of Japan|ja|第二国立銀行}}. The right of the front features Tamichi.]] {{main|National Bank Notes of Japan#One Yen Note}} In 1873 ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 6), Tamichi was depicted on the one-yen convertible banknotes issued by the National Bank.<ref name="uemura">Uemura Takashi, ''Portraits on Banknotes in Modern History,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2015, pp. 66–67. ISBN 978-4-64-203845-4</ref>
Two theories exist regarding the design of the note: one suggests it depicts Kamitsukeno no Tamichi fending off a surprise attack by the Emishi, while the other posits it as depicting {{ill|Minamoto no Tametomo|ja|源為朝}} defending against Kudo Shigemitsu’s fleet. Notably, the figure is depicted with a disproportionately large head relative to the body, an unusual stylistic choice.<ref name="uemura"/>
== Legend == Legends about Tamichi are preserved at the {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} in {{ill|Hirakawa, Aomori|ja|平川市}}, {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}, and the {{ill|Toriya Shrine|ja|鳥屋神社}} in {{ill|Ishinomaki|ja|石巻市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}.<ref>http://saruka.webcrow.jp/</ref><ref>https://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010081</ref>
== Genealogy == According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' Tamichi was the son of {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}} and the younger brother of {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}.
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volume 11, Emperor Nintoku’s 55th Year.
== Notes == {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of pre-Asuka period people|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}} * {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}} * {{ill|Tamichi Town|ja|田道町}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox noble | name = Kamitsukeno no Tamichi | image = Kamitsukenu-Tamichi.jpg | image_size = 220px | caption = {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}’s depiction of Tamichi in ''{{ill|Zenken Kojitsu|ja|前賢故実}}'' | era = {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Tamichi General | honorific_suffix = | deified_as = | grave = | rank = | allegiance = {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}} | clan = {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|下毛野氏|1=Shimotsukeno clan|lt=Shimotsukeno clan}}, ancestor of the Tomi no Muraji | parents = {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}}<ref name="nakata">''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}''</ref> | siblings = {{ill|Takehase no Mikoto|ja|竹葉瀬命}}<ref name="nakata"/> | spouse = | notable_works = }}
'''Kamitsukeno no Tamichi''' (田道, birth date unknown - 55th year of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}’s reign) was a warrior of the Nintoku court. His clan and title are not described in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', but the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' refers to him as '''Tamichi no Kimi''' (田道公) with the kabane title of ''Kimi''.
== Overview ==
In the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} neglected its tribute obligations. Tamichi’s elder brother, {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}, was dispatched as an envoy to investigate. Later, Tamichi led a military force to defeat the Silla troops. During the campaign, he captured and brought back the inhabitants of four settlements.
In the 55th year of Nintoku's reign, Tamichi was sent to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, but he was defeated and killed at the Ishinomito waterway. After his death, his attendant gave Tamichi’s keepsake, a jade he had wrapped around his hand, to his wife, who, stricken with grief, took her own life. Later, when the Emishi desecrated Tamichi’s grave, a giant serpent emerged and attacked them, causing many fatalities.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kudō Masaki|title=The Ancient History of the Emishi|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=2019|page=71}}</ref>
== Deification ==
Tamichi, who was sent north to suppress the Emishi, was later venerated as a deity of frontier development in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}} and {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北}}.<ref>https://toyohirajinja.or.jp/</ref> Major shrines dedicated to him include: * {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} * {{ill|Toyohira Shrine|ja|豊平神社}} * {{ill|Shinano Shrine|ja|信濃神社}}
== Banknote Depiction == [[File:Early one yen banknote front and reverse.jpg|thumb|Front and reverse of an early one-yen banknote issued by the {{ill|Second National Bank of Japan|ja|第二国立銀行}}. The right of the front features Tamichi.]] {{main|National Bank Notes of Japan#One Yen Note}} In 1873 ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 6), Tamichi was depicted on the one-yen convertible banknotes issued by the National Bank.<ref name="uemura">Uemura Takashi, ''Portraits on Banknotes in Modern History,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2015, pp. 66–67. ISBN 978-4-64-203845-4</ref>
Two theories exist regarding the design of the note: one suggests it depicts Kamitsukeno no Tamichi fending off a surprise attack by the Emishi, while the other posits it as depicting {{ill|Minamoto no Tametomo|ja|源為朝}} defending against Kudo Shigemitsu’s fleet. Notably, the figure is depicted with a disproportionately large head relative to the body, an unusual stylistic choice.<ref name="uemura"/>
== Legend == Legends about Tamichi are preserved at the {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社}} in {{ill|Hirakawa, Aomori|ja|平川市}}, {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}, and the {{ill|Toriya Shrine|ja|鳥屋神社}} in {{ill|Ishinomaki|ja|石巻市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}.<ref>http://saruka.webcrow.jp/</ref><ref>https://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010081</ref>
== Genealogy == According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' Tamichi was the son of {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命}} and the younger brother of {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬}}.
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volume 11, Emperor Nintoku’s 55th Year.
== Notes == {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of pre-Asuka period people|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}} * {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}} * {{ill|Tamichi Town|ja|田道町}}

{{Infobox noble | name = Kamitsukeno no Tamichi | image = Kamitsukenu-Tamichi.jpg | image_size = 220px | caption = {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎|12=draft}}’s depiction of Tamichi in ''{{ill|Zenken Kojitsu|ja|前賢故実|12=draft}}'' | era = {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}} | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Tamichi General | honorific_suffix = | deified_as = | grave = | rank = | allegiance = {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇|12=draft}} | clan = {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|下毛野氏|1=Shimotsukeno clan|lt=Shimotsukeno clan|12=draft}}, ancestor of the Tomi no Muraji | parents = {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命|12=draft}}<ref name="nakata">''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}''</ref> | siblings = {{ill|Takehase no Mikoto|ja|竹葉瀬命|12=draft}}<ref name="nakata"/> | spouse = | notable_works = }}
'''Kamitsukeno no Tamichi''' (田道, birth date unknown - 55th year of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇|12=draft}}’s reign) was a warrior of the Nintoku court. His clan and title are not described in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}'', but the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|12=draft}}'' refers to him as '''Tamichi no Kimi''' (田道公) with the kabane title of ''Kimi''.
== Overview ==
In the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅|12=draft}} neglected its tribute obligations. Tamichi’s elder brother, {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬|12=draft}}, was dispatched as an envoy to investigate. Later, Tamichi led a military force to defeat the Silla troops. During the campaign, he captured and brought back the inhabitants of four settlements.
In the 55th year of Nintoku's reign, Tamichi was sent to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷|12=draft}}, but he was defeated and killed at the Ishinomito waterway. After his death, his attendant gave Tamichi’s keepsake, a jade he had wrapped around his hand, to his wife, who, stricken with grief, took her own life. Later, when the Emishi desecrated Tamichi’s grave, a giant serpent emerged and attacked them, causing many fatalities.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kudō Masaki|title=The Ancient History of the Emishi|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft}}|year=2019|page=71}}</ref>
== Deification ==
Tamichi, who was sent north to suppress the Emishi, was later venerated as a deity of frontier development in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北|12=draft}}.<ref>https://toyohirajinja.or.jp/</ref> Major shrines dedicated to him include: * {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社|12=draft}} * {{ill|Toyohira Shrine|ja|豊平神社|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shinano Shrine|ja|信濃神社|12=draft}}
== Banknote Depiction == [[File:Early one yen banknote front and reverse.jpg|thumb|Front and reverse of an early one-yen banknote issued by the {{ill|Second National Bank of Japan|ja|第二国立銀行|12=draft}}. The right of the front features Tamichi.]] {{main|National Bank Notes of Japan#One Yen Note}} In 1873 ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|12=draft}} 6), Tamichi was depicted on the one-yen convertible banknotes issued by the National Bank.<ref name="uemura">Uemura Takashi, ''Portraits on Banknotes in Modern History,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2015, pp. 66–67. ISBN 978-4-64-203845-4</ref>
Two theories exist regarding the design of the note: one suggests it depicts Kamitsukeno no Tamichi fending off a surprise attack by the Emishi, while the other posits it as depicting {{ill|Minamoto no Tametomo|ja|源為朝|12=draft}} defending against Kudo Shigemitsu’s fleet. Notably, the figure is depicted with a disproportionately large head relative to the body, an unusual stylistic choice.<ref name="uemura"/>
== Legend == Legends about Tamichi are preserved at the {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社|12=draft}} in {{ill|Hirakawa, Aomori|ja|平川市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|12=draft}}, and the {{ill|Toriya Shrine|ja|鳥屋神社|12=draft}} in {{ill|Ishinomaki|ja|石巻市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft}}.<ref>http://saruka.webcrow.jp/</ref><ref>https://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010081</ref>
== Genealogy == According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' Tamichi was the son of {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命|12=draft}} and the younger brother of {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬|12=draft}}.
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volume 11, Emperor Nintoku’s 55th Year.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|List of pre-Asuka period people|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|12=draft}} * {{ill|Tamichi Town|ja|田道町|12=draft}}

'''Kamitsukeno no Tamichi''' (田道, birth date unknown - 55th year of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}’s reign) was a warrior of the Nintoku court. ISBN 978-4-64-203845-4</ref>
Two theories exist regarding the design of the note: one suggests it depicts Kamitsukeno no Tamichi fending off a surprise attack by the Emishi, while the other posits it as depicting {{ill|Minamoto no Tametomo|ja|源為朝|12=draft|12=draft}} defending against Kudo Shigemitsu’s fleet. Tamichi, who was sent north to suppress the Emishi, was later venerated as a deity of frontier development in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>https://toyohirajinja.or.jp/</ref> Major shrines dedicated to him include: * {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Toyohira Shrine|ja|豊平神社|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shinano Shrine|ja|信濃神社|12=draft|12=draft}}
== Banknote Depiction == [[File:Early one yen banknote front and reverse.jpg|thumb|Front and reverse of an early one-yen banknote issued by the {{ill|Second National Bank of Japan|ja|第二国立銀行|12=draft|12=draft}}. The right of the front features Tamichi.]] {{main|National Bank Notes of Japan#One Yen Note}} In 1873 ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|12=draft|12=draft}} 6), Tamichi was depicted on the one-yen convertible banknotes issued by the National Bank.<ref name="uemura">Uemura Takashi, ''Portraits on Banknotes in Modern History,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2015, pp. 66–67. ISBN 978-4-64-203845-4</ref>
Two theories exist regarding the design of the note: one suggests it depicts Kamitsukeno no Tamichi fending off a surprise attack by the Emishi, while the other posits it as depicting {{ill|Minamoto no Tametomo|ja|源為朝|12=draft|12=draft}} defending against Kudo Shigemitsu’s fleet. Notably, the figure is depicted with a disproportionately large head relative to the body, an unusual stylistic choice.<ref name="uemura"/>
== Legend == Legends about Tamichi are preserved at the {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Hirakawa, Aomori|ja|平川市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|12=draft|12=draft}}, and the {{ill|Toriya Shrine|ja|鳥屋神社|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Ishinomaki|ja|石巻市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>http://saruka.webcrow.jp/</ref><ref>https://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010081</ref>
== Genealogy == According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' Tamichi was the son of {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命|12=draft|12=draft}} and the younger brother of {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬|12=draft|12=draft}}.
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volume 11, Emperor Nintoku’s 55th Year.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|List of pre-Asuka period people|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Tamichi Town|ja|田道町|12=draft|12=draft}}

{{Infobox noble | name = Kamitsukeno no Tamichi | image = Kamitsukenu-Tamichi.jpg | image_size = 220px | caption = {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎|12=draft|12=draft}}’s depiction of Tamichi in ''{{ill|Zenken Kojitsu|ja|前賢故実|12=draft|12=draft}}'' | era = {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft|12=draft}} | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Tamichi General | honorific_suffix = | deified_as = | grave = | rank = | allegiance = {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇|12=draft|12=draft}} | clan = {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|下毛野氏|1=Shimotsukeno clan|lt=Shimotsukeno clan|12=draft|12=draft}}, ancestor of the Tomi no Muraji | parents = {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="nakata">''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}''</ref> | siblings = {{ill|Takehase no Mikoto|ja|竹葉瀬命|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="nakata"/> | spouse = | notable_works = }}
'''Kamitsukeno no Tamichi''' (田道, birth date unknown - 55th year of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}’s reign) was a warrior of the Nintoku court. His clan and title are not described in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}'', but the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|12=draft|12=draft}}'' refers to him as '''Tamichi no Kimi''' (田道公) with the kabane title of ''Kimi''.
== Overview ==
In the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅|12=draft|12=draft}} neglected its tribute obligations. Tamichi’s elder brother, {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬|12=draft|12=draft}}, was dispatched as an envoy to investigate. Later, Tamichi led a military force to defeat the Silla troops. During the campaign, he captured and brought back the inhabitants of four settlements.
In the 55th year of Nintoku's reign, Tamichi was sent to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷|12=draft|12=draft}}, but he was defeated and killed at the Ishinomito waterway. After his death, his attendant gave Tamichi’s keepsake, a jade he had wrapped around his hand, to his wife, who, stricken with grief, took her own life. Later, when the Emishi desecrated Tamichi’s grave, a giant serpent emerged and attacked them, causing many fatalities.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kudō Masaki|title=The Ancient History of the Emishi|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft|12=draft}}|year=2019|page=71}}</ref>
== Deification ==
Tamichi, who was sent north to suppress the Emishi, was later venerated as a deity of frontier development in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>https://toyohirajinja.or.jp/</ref> Major shrines dedicated to him include: * {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Toyohira Shrine|ja|豊平神社|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shinano Shrine|ja|信濃神社|12=draft|12=draft}}
== Banknote Depiction == [[File:Early one yen banknote front and reverse.jpg|thumb|Front and reverse of an early one-yen banknote issued by the {{ill|Second National Bank of Japan|ja|第二国立銀行|12=draft|12=draft}}. The right of the front features Tamichi.]] {{main|National Bank Notes of Japan#One Yen Note}} In 1873 ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|12=draft|12=draft}} 6), Tamichi was depicted on the one-yen convertible banknotes issued by the National Bank.<ref name="uemura">Uemura Takashi, ''Portraits on Banknotes in Modern History,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2015, pp. 66–67. ISBN 978-4-64-203845-4</ref>
Two theories exist regarding the design of the note: one suggests it depicts Kamitsukeno no Tamichi fending off a surprise attack by the Emishi, while the other posits it as depicting {{ill|Minamoto no Tametomo|ja|源為朝|12=draft|12=draft}} defending against Kudo Shigemitsu’s fleet. Notably, the figure is depicted with a disproportionately large head relative to the body, an unusual stylistic choice.<ref name="uemura"/>
== Legend == Legends about Tamichi are preserved at the {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Hirakawa, Aomori|ja|平川市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|12=draft|12=draft}}, and the {{ill|Toriya Shrine|ja|鳥屋神社|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Ishinomaki|ja|石巻市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>http://saruka.webcrow.jp/</ref><ref>https://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010081</ref>
== Genealogy == According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' Tamichi was the son of {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命|12=draft|12=draft}} and the younger brother of {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬|12=draft|12=draft}}.
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volume 11, Emperor Nintoku’s 55th Year.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|List of pre-Asuka period people|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Tamichi Town|ja|田道町|12=draft|12=draft}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox noble | name = Kamitsukeno no Tamichi | image = Kamitsukenu-Tamichi.jpg | image_size = 220px | caption = {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎|12=draft|12=draft}}’s depiction of Tamichi in ''{{ill|Zenken Kojitsu|ja|前賢故実|12=draft|12=draft}}'' | era = {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft|12=draft}} | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Tamichi General | honorific_suffix = | deified_as = | grave = | rank = | allegiance = {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇|12=draft|12=draft}} | clan = {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|下毛野氏|1=Shimotsukeno clan|lt=Shimotsukeno clan|12=draft|12=draft}}, ancestor of the Tomi no Muraji | parents = {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="nakata">''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}''</ref> | siblings = {{ill|Takehase no Mikoto|ja|竹葉瀬命|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="nakata"/> | spouse = | notable_works = }}
'''Kamitsukeno no Tamichi''' (田道, birth date unknown - 55th year of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}’s reign) was a warrior of the Nintoku court. His clan and title are not described in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}'', but the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|12=draft|12=draft}}'' refers to him as '''Tamichi no Kimi''' (田道公) with the kabane title of ''Kimi''.
== Overview ==
In the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅|12=draft|12=draft}} neglected its tribute obligations. Tamichi’s elder brother, {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬|12=draft|12=draft}}, was dispatched as an envoy to investigate. Later, Tamichi led a military force to defeat the Silla troops. During the campaign, he captured and brought back the inhabitants of four settlements.
In the 55th year of Nintoku's reign, Tamichi was sent to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷|12=draft|12=draft}}, but he was defeated and killed at the Ishinomito waterway. After his death, his attendant gave Tamichi’s keepsake, a jade he had wrapped around his hand, to his wife, who, stricken with grief, took her own life. Later, when the Emishi desecrated Tamichi’s grave, a giant serpent emerged and attacked them, causing many fatalities.<ref>{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kudō Masaki|title=The Ancient History of the Emishi|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft|12=draft}}|year=2019|page=71}}</ref>
== Deification ==
Tamichi, who was sent north to suppress the Emishi, was later venerated as a deity of frontier development in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>https://toyohirajinja.or.jp/</ref> Major shrines dedicated to him include: * {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Toyohira Shrine|ja|豊平神社|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shinano Shrine|ja|信濃神社|12=draft|12=draft}}
== Banknote Depiction == [[File:Early one yen banknote front and reverse.jpg|thumb|Front and reverse of an early one-yen banknote issued by the {{ill|Second National Bank of Japan|ja|第二国立銀行|12=draft|12=draft}}. The right of the front features Tamichi.]] {{main|National Bank Notes of Japan#One Yen Note}} In 1873 ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|12=draft|12=draft}} 6), Tamichi was depicted on the one-yen convertible banknotes issued by the National Bank.<ref name="uemura">Uemura Takashi, ''Portraits on Banknotes in Modern History,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2015, pp. 66–67. ISBN 978-4-64-203845-4</ref>
Two theories exist regarding the design of the note: one suggests it depicts Kamitsukeno no Tamichi fending off a surprise attack by the Emishi, while the other posits it as depicting {{ill|Minamoto no Tametomo|ja|源為朝|12=draft|12=draft}} defending against Kudo Shigemitsu’s fleet. Notably, the figure is depicted with a disproportionately large head relative to the body, an unusual stylistic choice.<ref name="uemura"/>
== Legend == Legends about Tamichi are preserved at the {{ill|Saruga Shrine|ja|猿賀神社|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Hirakawa, Aomori|ja|平川市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|12=draft|12=draft}}, and the {{ill|Toriya Shrine|ja|鳥屋神社|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Ishinomaki|ja|石巻市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>http://saruka.webcrow.jp/</ref><ref>https://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010081</ref>
== Genealogy == According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' Tamichi was the son of {{ill|Ōaratabe no Mikoto|ja|大荒田別命|12=draft|12=draft}} and the younger brother of {{ill|Takehase|ja|竹葉瀬|12=draft|12=draft}}.
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volume 11, Emperor Nintoku’s 55th Year.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|List of pre-Asuka period people|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Tamichi Town|ja|田道町|12=draft|12=draft}}

ISBN 978-4-64-203845-4</ref>
